topic: IO - Temporary File Patterns Without External Crates

turns.content: I'm stuck on my CI pipeline because my Rust tests keep failing when running in parallel. I thought using std::env::temp_dir() to get the OS temp directory and just appending a timestamp to the filename would work, but it seems like that's not enough to prevent race conditions.

That's a common first guess, but relying only on a timestamp isn't safe because two threads can generate the same name at the exact same millisecond. You need something more unique and atomic. The standard approach is to combine a thread ID from std::thread::current().id() with a high-resolution timestamp, then use an atomic write pattern where you write to a temp file first and only rename it once the data is fully flushed.

So I should create a custom RAII wrapper that holds the path to a temporary file in the OS temp directory, writes the data atomically into it, and then renames it to the final location? And how do I ensure the cleanup happens automatically when the scope ends without needing an external crate like tempfile?

Exactly. You can define your own struct that implements Drop so it performs a remove operation on the file when it goes out of scope, acting as a manual RAII guard. Inside, you'll implement a method to open the file with std::fs::OpenOptions, write using BufWriter for performance, and call rename at the end using std::os::unix::fs::rename if running on Unix-like systems or fallback logic for Windows. This avoids the overhead of crates like tempfile while keeping the safety guarantees.

I'm worried about thread safety since multiple tests might run concurrently. Should I use std::sync::Mutex around my temp file creation? That seems like it would add too much locking overhead and slow down the CI pipeline significantly.

You don't need a mutex for this pattern because each test instance creates its own unique path with the thread ID, so files never overlap. The atomic write-then-rename ensures that only one writer touches a given file at any moment since the filename is unique per invocation. If you do want shared access later to read results, use std::sync::Mutex around the actual data storage, not the temporary file creation itself.

Got it, but what about error handling if the OS temp directory is full or permissions are denied? Should I panic in production code or implement custom error propagation using Result types and std::io::Error variants?

In a library context you'd propagate errors using std::io::Result and let callers decide, but for CI scripts returning a non-zero exit code is often cleaner. You can check if the temp directory exists with std::fs::metadata before creating files, and handle permission issues by logging them instead of crashing. For production Rust apps, wrapping these in custom error types that extend std::io::Error makes debugging much easier without bloating your binary.

Thanks for clarifying the atomic rename part. One last thing—can I simplify this further by just using a simple closure with capture? I want to avoid writing a full struct and just inline everything into a function that returns an Option<PathBuf> when successful.

You can absolutely do this, but be careful not to lose the Drop semantics if you're reusing files. A closure returning Option is great for one-off uses where you don't need guaranteed cleanup on scope exit. If your CI pipeline runs tests in parallel and wants consistent teardown behavior across all invocations, sticking with a struct that implements Drop ensures every temporary file gets removed even if an exception occurs during the write phase.
Perfect, I'll implement the RAII wrapper this weekend and re-run the test suite. Hopefully my CI pipeline will finally pass without those flaky race conditions.
That sounds like a solid plan. Just remember to add some debug logging at the rename step so you can verify that files are actually being swapped correctly in your logs before merging.
